original_abstract:
This chapter examines the complex and often enigmatic relationship between religious faith and public policy during Tony Blair’s premiership. While Blair’s personal Christian convictions were a defining feature of his political identity, they remained a source of public tension, oscillating between explicit moral vision and a strategic reticence to "do God" in an increasingly secular Britain. The author argues that New Labour's project was rooted in an effort to "remoralise" Britain—a trajectory influenced by the legacy of ethical socialism and the preceding "conviction politics" of the Thatcher era.

Beyond the personal faith of political leaders, the text explores the structural role of religion in the public sphere, addressing the "new visibility" of faith-based organizations as key players in social welfare and urban regeneration. The author critiques the classical liberal requirement that religious reasoning be privatized, drawing on the work of José Casanova and Jürgen Habermas to propose a "post-secular" framework for public discourse. By distinguishing between "programmatic" and "procedural" secularism, the paper suggests that a healthy pluralist democracy should not silence religious voices but rather mediate them through a shared civil language. Ultimately, the chapter calls for increased "religious literacy" to better analyze how theological narratives continue to shape political identity, institutional values, and the concept of the common good in a diverse society.
humanized_abstract:
Chapter 6 examines the dynamic between religious faith and public policy under the leadership of Tony Blair. This was a presidency dominated by one individual whose commitment to Christian faith played a distinctive part in his personal politics. This relationship with the public appeared to veer between Blair’s assertion of a clear moral purpose to his political ambitions, and an awkward reluctance to make too much of his Christianity in a country that he often appeared to think had no religion. In the context of New Labour this attempt to ‘remoralise’ Britain can be seen within the ethical socialism tradition and also as part of the legacy of Thatcher’s ‘conviction politics’.

This chapter covers two aspects of religion and the politics of the common good in Europe, other than political leaders’ individual religious beliefs. It starts with the new visibility of religious actors and the questions it raises. The underlying classical liberal assumption that religious beliefs and motivations have to be relegated to private lives and may not be applicable in the public sphere, is contested in the light of theories of José Casanova and Jürgen Habermas referring to the post-secular circumstances of today’s democracies. Programmatic secularism with its tendency to exclude religion from the public sphere, on the one hand, and the procedural secularism that bridges this divide by demanding the translation of religious and secular discourses into a language that is intelligible for all citizens, on the other hand, should ensure that plurality does not imply the exclusion of religion from the political public space. What is required for all participants – in particular also for those bearing responsibility for major institutions – is religious literacy and a sounder understanding of how political beliefs are framed in religious discourses and how these in turn structure the bases of political organisations and the institutions they manage. The underlying issue is how to ensure the continuation of religion-inspired narratives in forming political and institutional identities, as well as the definition of the common good.
